title: Nine Inquiries Under the Same Raghupathi?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil reading copy below was checked against the source-page images: the opening salutation omitted by OCR was restored, the damaged area figure was restored as 10,375.7 square metres, and clear OCR spacing and character errors were corrected. The canonical source document remains unchanged. Reports, allegations, and questions in the letter are translated as attributed claims, not presented as independently established facts. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has appointed an inquiry commission headed by Justice Raghupathi to investigate the collapse of an eleven-storey building in Chennai, which has so far claimed 61 lives. If an incident like this had occurred when the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam was in office, just imagine the statements Jayalalithaa would have issued! The moment news came that ten people had died, she would have poured down a rain of condemnation: "Karunanidhi, Chief Minister of the minority government, must accept responsibility, resign immediately and leave office." With demonstrations and road blockades, she would have thrown the entire town into turmoil. The newspapers, too, would have sung her praises! But now, a week after the disaster, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has announced a one-person commission.

Even the pro-government magazine *Junior Vikatan* (6 July 2014) began a lengthy article by saying: "A massive human tragedy has occurred in Chennai. When the entire site is cleared, the death toll may be staggering. Priceless human lives have been sacrificed by the combination of money-hungry officials and greedy builders. Those in power are trying to escape by blaming nature: wind, rain, thunder and lightning. In doing so, they seek to conceal their own self-serving conduct. But the Moulivakkam tragedy has brought out a stench of corruption even stronger than the stench of death."

It is fortunate, at least, that the building collapsed while it was still under construction. Had the same thing happened after construction was complete and all the buyers had moved in, how many people would have perished? It is impossible even to imagine! Has the government ruling the State given any thought to this? It has happily convened its executive committee and passed seven resolutions praising the Chief Minister. When 61 people have died and been buried under the earth during the AIADMK regime, should it not pass a resolution praising the Chief Minister for that as well?

In Chennai, any residential complex with more than two floors in addition to the ground floor must receive approval from the Chennai Metropolitan Development Authority, the CMDA. There are several subsidiary rules governing the implementation of this requirement. Do CMDA officials enforce all those rules properly? Who was the minister responsible for the department when this building was approved? With 61 people dead, what action has the government taken against that minister? If another government had been in office, would they not have demanded even the Chief Minister's resignation? Did the departmental minister sign the file granting permission for this building, or did he not?

Even after the CMDA grants approval, its officials are required to visit the site at every stage of construction and ensure that the building conforms to the details in the planning permission. Did they do so? Or did they simply collect whatever had to be collected each time and return?

The application submitted for this building states that the total site area was 111,684 square feet, or 10,375.7 square metres. After excluding the areas intended for open space, a park and a swimming pool, permission was sought to construct two eleven-storey buildings on 3,986.17 square metres. It is said that when the application was first submitted to the Planning Permission Section, neither a soil-resource certification nor the no-objection certificates from the relevant departments were attached. Is it true that the company constructing the building began this work only after first meeting and speaking to the departmental minister, and that this was why it submitted the application without the important certificates that should have accompanied it?

Later, they reportedly obtained a soil-resource certificate from some engineer and filed it. When the committee members met to consider approval, it was reportedly pointed out that the road was not as wide as shown in the plan and that adequate open space had not been left along the sides of the building. Yet it is said that a decision was taken after the departmental minister stated that the relevant rules could be relaxed. Is this also true?

Planning permission was granted to this company only on 3 June 2013. But during the year in which construction was under way, did any CMDA official visit the site and conduct an inspection? It is said that when work first began, some social workers from the area wrote complaints saying, "This is clay soil. Do not permit a multi-storey building there. Cancel the permission already granted." Is that true or not?

Is it true or not that the government granted permission despite all this and is now trying to save itself by placing the blame on the company, alleging that it violated the rules and did not build according to the planning approval?

After the building collapsed, it was reported in the newspapers that the Chief Minister asked the government adviser for a complete report on how permission had been granted; that the current CMDA Member-Secretary explained how rules had previously been relaxed to grant approval; and that, when questioned, the official who had held the post at the time explained that he had been obliged to act on the minister's instructions. Are all these reports true or not?

Even before determining whether these matters are true, and even before the newly appointed one-person commission could investigate and reach a conclusion, was it proper for the Chief Minister to visit the site and tell reporters, "There was no error in the CMDA's approval; permission was granted properly; the construction company built the structure in violation of the rules"? When the Chief Minister has already said this, how can the investigating judge express a different view? Does this not amount to instructing the judge of the inquiry commission on what conclusion to give?

The newspaper *Dinamalar* asked why two Government Orders had been issued relaxing the rules for the collapsed building. For writing this, the government advocate hurriedly filed a case against the newspaper. Does that not amount to intimidating newspapers that report the truth?

It was also reported that rescue operations were halted for several hours after word came that Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa would visit the site. Newspapers further reported that when journalists asked her about this, the Chief Minister refused to answer, saying the question carried a political ulterior motive.

A soil-testing expert has said: "At Moulivakkam, where the accident occurred, there is clay soil to a depth of 25 metres. Therefore, a pile foundation should have been laid at a depth of 26 metres. Had that been done, the building would not have collapsed in this manner." Did CMDA officials pay attention to any of this?

After inspecting the rescue work, Andhra Pradesh Chief Minister Chandrababu Naidu said, "This building was constructed in violation of the rules. Strict action must therefore be taken against those responsible."

The National Human Rights Commission has taken suo motu cognisance of the collapse and sent a notice to the Tamil Nadu government seeking an explanation. The notice sent to the Chief Secretary states: "On the basis of reports about the accident in newspapers and television, and the interview given by the Chief Minister, the National Human Rights Commission has taken up this matter. The Commission considers this a grave human-rights violation. Treating it as an act that deprived those killed and injured in the accident of their rights, the Tamil Nadu government must submit its explanation regarding the accident within two weeks."

Union Urban Development Minister Venkaiah Naidu said yesterday: "The laws governing multi-storey buildings will soon be amended. In future, officials themselves will have to bear responsibility when multi-storey buildings collapse."

It is in these circumstances that the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister has announced a one-person inquiry commission. Approval for this building was granted by the CMDA, which is under the control of the Tamil Nadu government. The person responsible for it is a member of the Tamil Nadu Cabinet. When numerous allegations concern those very authorities and their approval of this building, one must consider how appropriate it is for the Tamil Nadu government itself to appoint a judge and claim that it will investigate the matter.

There is a widely expressed view that no truth will emerge from the one-person commission now constituted by Jayalalithaa. The Chief Minister has announced the commission but has not stated how many days it has to investigate and submit its report. That alone leaves one to conclude that it is an eyewash commission, appointed to enable the government to escape responsibility.

As soon as the AIADMK came to power in Tamil Nadu in 2011, Jayalalithaa alleged irregularities in the construction of the new Secretariat complex, which had been built well during the Kazhagam government, and appointed Justice Thangaraj to investigate. When he stepped down from that position within a few months, Justice Raghupathi was appointed in his place and has continued to carry out that work. The same Justice Raghupathi is also the judge of the body that currently considers cases under the Goondas Act. Not only that, the same Raghupathi also serves as a consumer-court judge. While he already holds all these responsibilities, he has now also been appointed to investigate the Moulivakkam building collapse. From this alone, it is clear that this inquiry has been constituted with an ulterior motive and as an eyewash.

If the collapse of this building has so far killed 61 people, those governing the State, the Chief Minister and the minister responsible for this department must accept responsibility. If all the facts are to emerge about the irregularities that occurred and those who benefited from them, a CBI investigation would be the appropriate course. Otherwise, it can only be concluded that this government has appointed an eyewash commission.
With affection,
M. K.
5 July 2014
